original_abstract:
In this work, we introduce redMaPPer, a next-generation red-sequence cluster-finding algorithm optimized for the requirements of current and upcoming wide-field photometric surveys. The algorithm incorporates several key innovations: it utilizes iterative self-training of the red-sequence model using minimal spectroscopic data—a vital capability for high-redshift regimes—and robustly accounts for complex masks with varying survey depth. To facilitate large-scale structure studies, the code generates cluster-appropriate random points. Each detected cluster is assigned a full redshift probability distribution, $P(z)$, and multiple potential central galaxies, each with an associated centering probability. Furthermore, the algorithm is numerically efficient and highly parallelizable, capable of processing a Dark Energy Survey-scale catalog in approximately 500 CPU hours. 

We demonstrate that redMaPPer yields exceptional photometric redshift accuracy, produces richness estimates that correlate strongly with external mass proxies, and achieves high levels of completeness and purity. Applying the algorithm to roughly 10,000 deg$^2$ of SDSS DR8 data, we present a catalog of approximately 25,000 clusters across the redshift range $0.08 < z < 0.55$. The resulting photometric redshifts are nearly Gaussian, with a scatter of $\sigma_z \approx 0.006$ at $z \sim 0.1$, rising to $\sigma_z \approx 0.02$ at $z \sim 0.5$ as photometric noise increases near the survey limit. The median $|\Delta z| / (1+z)$ for the entire sample is 0.006, and the incidence of projection effects remains low ($\le 5\%$). Detailed performance benchmarks comparing the redMaPPer DR8 catalog to X-ray and Sunyaev-Zel'dovich (SZ) samples are provided in a companion paper.
humanized_abstract:
We present redMaPPer, a new cluster finding algorithm for use in future survey generations. redMaPPer combines the self-calibrating, non-orthogonal approach of maxBCG with more sophisticated treatment of the red sequence model and cluster centering, all in a parallelized code that may be run on a small cluster or a modest number of nodes on a supercomputer. redMaPPer has two main advantages for surveys such as DES: (1) it includes an iterative self-calibration of the red sequence model, using as few spectra as possible, to facilitate cluster detection at very large redshifts, and (2) it is able to handle masks which are complicated by inhomogeneous coverage. The algorithm generates cluster-random catalogs for use in large scale structure studies. It yields a full probability distribution function $P(z)$ for each cluster detection, along with multiple candidate central galaxies with corresponding probabilities of being the true center. We run the code on a moderate cluster to demonstrate speed and the robustness of the final catalog. The entire DES catalog is processed in less than 500 CPU hours of computing time.

Using advanced techniques, we have applied the algorithm, and shown that redMaPPer clusters have very accurate photometric redshifts, with highly correlated richness measurements with respect to both X-ray and SZ mass. We also find cluster to be highly complete and pure. In this paper, we apply the algorithm to the 10 000 deg^2 of Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) DR8, and present a rich and large cluster catalogue for 25000 clusters at $0.08 < z < 0.55$. The distributions of our photometric redshifts are almost Gaussian, with scatter $\sigma_z \approx 0.006$ at $z \sim 0.1$, and increases as we move towards the fainter parts of the survey, giving $\sigma_z \approx 0.02$ at $z \sim 0.5$. The median $|\Delta z| / (1+z)$ of our sample is 0.006, and we also find that contamination of cluster samples by smaller galaxies and stars, due to projection, is less than $5\%$ more than than on average. More details on redMaPPer performance, measured against X-ray, as well as SZ data are given in the next paper in this series.

document.output:
We present redMaPPer, a new cluster finding algorithm for use in future survey generations. redMaPPer combines the self-calibrating, non-orthogonal approach of maxBCG with more sophisticated treatment of the red sequence model and cluster centering, all in a parallelized code that may be run on a small cluster or a modest number of nodes on a supercomputer. redMaPPer has two main advantages for surveys such as DES: (1) it includes an iterative self-calibration of the red sequence model, using as few spectra as possible, to facilitate cluster detection at very large redshifts, and (2) it is able to handle masks which are complicated by inhomogeneous coverage. The algorithm generates cluster-random catalogs for use in large scale structure studies. It yields a full probability distribution function $P(z)$ for each cluster detection, along with multiple candidate central galaxies with corresponding probabilities of being the true center. We run the code on a moderate cluster to demonstrate speed and the robustness of the final catalog. The entire DES catalog is processed in less than 500 CPU hours of computing time.

Using advanced techniques, we have applied the algorithm, and shown that redMaPPer clusters have very accurate photometric redshifts, with highly correlated richness measurements with respect to both X-ray and SZ mass. We also find cluster to be highly complete and pure. In this paper, we apply the algorithm to the 10 000 deg^2 of Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) DR8, and present a rich and large cluster catalogue for 25000 clusters at $0.08 < z < 0.55$. The distributions of our photometric redshifts are almost Gaussian, with scatter $\sigma_z \approx 0.006$ at $z \sim 0.1$, and increases as we move towards the fainter parts of the survey, giving $\sigma_z \approx 0.02$ at $z \sim 0.5$. The median $|\Delta z| / (1+z)$ of our sample is 0.006, and we also find that contamination of cluster samples by smaller galaxies and stars, due to projection, is less than $5\%$ more than than on average. More details on redMaPPer performance, measured against X-ray, as well as SZ data are given in the next paper in this series.
document.input:
In this work, we introduce redMaPPer, a next-generation red-sequence cluster-finding algorithm optimized for the requirements of current and upcoming wide-field photometric surveys.
